title: Articles and gender
rule_text: Nouns are usually masculine or feminine. Common pattern: nouns ending in -o are often masculine and nouns ending in -a are often feminine. Use definite articles el, la, los, las and indefinite articles un, una, unos, unas. Adjectives often agree with the noun in gender and number.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong article with a noun
Forgetting adjective agreement

title: Singular and plural
rule_text: Add -s after a vowel, add -es after most consonants, and change z to c before adding -es.
common_mistakes: Adding the wrong plural ending
Not changing z to c

title: Subject pronouns
rule_text: Spanish subject pronouns are yo, tu, el, ella, usted, nosotros, nosotras, vosotros, vosotras, ellos, ellas, and ustedes. Spanish often drops the subject pronoun when the verb form is clear.
common_mistakes: Overusing subject pronouns in every sentence

title: Present tense basics
rule_text: Regular present tense endings are -o, -as, -a, -amos, -an for -ar verbs; -o, -es, -e, -emos, -en for -er verbs; and -o, -es, -e, -imos, -en for -ir verbs.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong ending for the verb type

title: Ser vs estar
rule_text: Use ser for identity, origin, characteristics, time, and relationships. Use estar for location, temporary states, and many feelings or conditions.
common_mistakes: Using ser for location
Using estar for permanent identity

title: Hay vs estar
rule_text: Use hay to say that something exists. Use estar to say where a specific thing is located.
examples: Hay un libro en la mesa.
El libro esta en la mesa.
common_mistakes: Using esta to introduce existence
Using hay for location of a specific object

title: Question words
rule_text: Common question words include que, quien, donde, cuando, por que, como, and cual.
common_mistakes: Choosing the wrong interrogative word

title: Gustar pattern
rule_text: Gustar works like 'is pleasing to'. Use gusta with a singular noun or infinitive and gustan with plural nouns.
common_mistakes: Treating gustar like English like
Using gusta with plural nouns

title: Negation
rule_text: Put no before the verb. Words like nunca, nadie, and nada also commonly appear with no before the verb.
common_mistakes: Putting no in the wrong position

title: Adjective agreement
rule_text: Adjectives usually match the noun in gender and number.
common_mistakes: Leaving adjectives in masculine singular form

title: Possessives
rule_text: Common possessives are mi, tu, su, nuestro, and vuestro. They agree in number when needed, such as mis libros and nuestras casas.
common_mistakes: Forgetting plural agreement in possessives

title: Direct object pronouns
rule_text: Direct object pronouns replace the noun receiving the action: me, te, lo, la, nos, os, los, las.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong object pronoun
Leaving the noun and pronoun together unnecessarily

title: Indirect object pronouns
rule_text: Indirect object pronouns show to whom or for whom something is done: me, te, le, nos, os, les.
examples: Doy el libro a Ana. -> Le doy el libro.
common_mistakes: Confusing direct and indirect object pronouns

title: Common present irregulars
rule_text: Common present tense irregulars include tengo, hago, pongo, salgo, vengo, digo, oigo, and conozco.
common_mistakes: Applying regular endings to irregular first-person forms

title: Stem-changing verbs
rule_text: Common stem changes are e to ie, o to ue, and e to i. The change usually appears in all present tense forms except nosotros and vosotros.
common_mistakes: Applying the stem change to nosotros forms

title: Reflexive verbs
rule_text: Reflexive verbs use me, te, se, nos, os, and se with the verb and are common for routines and self-directed actions.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the reflexive pronoun

title: Near future
rule_text: Use ir + a + infinitive to express 'going to' in the near future.
common_mistakes: Forgetting the preposition a

title: Simple past overview
rule_text: Use the preterite for completed actions. Use the imperfect for background, repeated past actions, age, time, and ongoing situations.
examples: Cuando era nino, estudiaba en casa.
common_mistakes: Using preterite when describing background context
Using imperfect for a clearly completed action

title: Por vs para
rule_text: Por often expresses cause, exchange, movement through, duration, or means. Para often expresses purpose, destination, deadline, or recipient.
common_mistakes: Using por when expressing purpose
Using para when expressing reason or exchange

title: Personal a
rule_text: Use a before a specific human direct object.
common_mistakes: Omitting the personal a with people

title: Comparisons
rule_text: Use mas ... que for 'more than', menos ... que for 'less than', and tan ... como for 'as ... as'. Mejor and peor are irregular comparisons.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong comparison pattern

title: Commands overview
rule_text: Informal tu commands are common: habla, come, vive. Negative tu commands use the present subjunctive: no hables, no comas, no vivas.
common_mistakes: Using affirmative tu forms in negative commands

title: Tener que, deber, and hay que
rule_text: Use tener que + infinitive for personal obligation, deber + infinitive for obligation or recommendation, and hay que + infinitive for a general impersonal obligation.
common_mistakes: Using hay que for a specific person
Confusing strong obligation with general advice

title: Preterite conjugation
rule_text: The preterite is used for completed past actions. Regular endings are -e, -aste, -o, -amos, -aron for -ar verbs and -i, -iste, -io, -imos, -ieron for -er and -ir verbs.
common_mistakes: Using present endings in past narration
Confusing preterite endings across verb groups

title: Imperfect conjugation
rule_text: The imperfect is used for ongoing, repeated, descriptive, or background past actions. Regular endings are -aba, -abas, -aba, -abamos, -aban for -ar verbs and -ia, -ias, -ia, -iamos, -ian for -er and -ir verbs.
common_mistakes: Using preterite for repeated background actions
Forgetting the imperfect endings

title: Preterite vs imperfect
rule_text: Use the preterite for completed, bounded past actions and the imperfect for background description, repeated habits, time, age, and ongoing situations in the past.
common_mistakes: Using imperfect for a one-time completed action
Using preterite for background description

title: Present progressive
rule_text: Use estar + gerundio to describe an action happening right now or in progress. Gerunds usually end in -ando or -iendo.
common_mistakes: Using ser instead of estar
Using the infinitive instead of a gerund

title: Subjunctive basics
rule_text: The present subjunctive is often used in subordinate clauses to express uncertainty, desire, emotion, recommendation, or non-factual situations. It is commonly triggered by que after certain expressions.
common_mistakes: Using the indicative after a subjunctive trigger
Forgetting that many subjunctive clauses begin with que

title: Subjunctive with wishes and emotions
rule_text: Use the subjunctive after expressions of desire, preference, command, or emotion such as quiero que, prefiero que, me alegra que, and temo que.
common_mistakes: Using indicative after quiero que
Missing the mood change after emotional expressions

title: Subjunctive with doubt and impersonal expressions
rule_text: Use the subjunctive after doubt, uncertainty, and many impersonal expressions such as es posible que, es importante que, and dudo que.
common_mistakes: Using indicative after impersonal necessity or possibility expressions

title: Formal and plural commands
rule_text: Formal usted, ustedes, and nosotros commands use subjunctive-based forms such as hable, hablen, and hablemos.
common_mistakes: Using tu command forms for usted or ustedes

title: Double object pronouns
rule_text: When a direct and indirect object pronoun appear together, the indirect object comes first. Le and les change to se before lo, la, los, or las.
common_mistakes: Using le lo instead of se lo
Reversing pronoun order

title: Pronoun placement
rule_text: Object and reflexive pronouns usually go before a conjugated verb, but they can attach to infinitives, gerunds, and affirmative commands.
common_mistakes: Putting pronouns after a conjugated verb
Forgetting attached pronouns on commands or infinitives

title: Relative pronouns
rule_text: Relative pronouns like que, quien, and lo que connect clauses and refer back to nouns, people, or ideas already mentioned.
common_mistakes: Choosing the wrong relative pronoun for people or abstract ideas

title: Demonstratives
rule_text: Use este, ese, and aquel and their feminine and plural forms to point out things based on relative distance from the speaker.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong form for gender or number
Ignoring the distance distinction

title: Superlatives
rule_text: Use el mas, la mas, los menos, and similar forms to express the highest or lowest degree. Absolute superlatives can also appear with endings like -isimo.
common_mistakes: Using comparative structures when a superlative is needed

title: Irregular comparatives
rule_text: Mejor, peor, mayor, and menor are common irregular comparative forms and often replace more literal comparative patterns.
common_mistakes: Using mas bueno instead of mejor in standard comparison contexts

title: Present perfect
rule_text: Use haber in the present tense plus a past participle to talk about actions connected to the present, such as experiences or recent events.
common_mistakes: Using ser or estar instead of haber
Using an infinitive instead of a participle

title: Past participles
rule_text: Regular past participles end in -ado for -ar verbs and -ido for -er and -ir verbs. Some common verbs have irregular participles, such as hecho, dicho, escrito, and visto.
common_mistakes: Using the wrong participle ending
Forgetting common irregular participles

title: Conditional basics
rule_text: The conditional expresses what would happen, polite requests, or softened statements. It is often formed by adding endings like -ia, -ias, -iamos to the infinitive.
common_mistakes: Confusing future and conditional endings

title: Simple future
rule_text: The simple future expresses what will happen and is formed by adding endings like -e, -as, -a, -emos, -an to the infinitive, with some irregular stems.
common_mistakes: Using present tense where future meaning should be explicit
Forgetting irregular stems like tendr-

title: Si clauses
rule_text: Si clauses express conditions. Real conditions often use si + present + future or imperative, while hypothetical conditions later use imperfect subjunctive plus conditional.
common_mistakes: Using future directly after si in a real condition

title: Passive and impersonal se
rule_text: Spanish often prefers se constructions over the full passive voice. Use se to make statements impersonal or to express passive meaning without naming the agent.
common_mistakes: Overusing the English-style passive
Confusing impersonal se with reflexive se

title: Se for unplanned events
rule_text: Spanish often uses se with an indirect object pronoun to describe accidental or unplanned events, shifting focus away from direct personal blame.
common_mistakes: Using a standard active construction where Spanish prefers accidental se

title: Accent marks and question marks
rule_text: Spanish uses written accent marks to distinguish pronunciation or meaning and uses both opening and closing question and exclamation marks.
common_mistakes: Omitting accent marks on common interrogatives
Forgetting opening punctuation

title: Numbers, dates, and time
rule_text: Spanish uses specific patterns for counting, giving dates, and telling time, including es la for one o'clock and son las for other hours.
examples: Hoy es el cinco de mayo.
common_mistakes: Using es for all times
Translating date formats too literally from English

title: Word order with adjectives and pronouns
rule_text: Spanish usually places most descriptive adjectives after the noun and places object pronouns before conjugated verbs or attached to infinitives, gerunds, and affirmative commands.
common_mistakes: Placing descriptive adjectives before the noun by default
Misplacing pronouns in the sentence

title: Common connectors
rule_text: Connectors such as porque, aunque, entonces, primero, despues, and sin embargo help link ideas and build more natural sentences and paragraphs.
common_mistakes: Overusing very basic connectors only
Choosing a connector that does not match the logical relationship
